item.title: Indian Exam Board Admits to Cybersecurity Flaws Found by Teen
item.description: A national school exam board in India said it has been monitoring and has contained vulnerabilities in its online grading portal for one of the country’s most important school-leaving exams that were first flagged by a teenage cybersecurity researcher.

item.title: Australia Home Prices Flatline as Rate Hikes, Tax Changes Weigh
item.description: Australian home prices stagnated last month, with growth weakening across most major cities as higher borrowing costs and tax reforms sapped demand.

item.title: Apple Seeks to Disrupt the Glasses Market the Way It Did With Watches
item.description: Also: The latest on iOS 27, iOS 28, new Apple TV and HomePod mini.

item.title: Big Tech Is Suddenly Impacting Niche Market for US Dividends
item.description: The dominance of a few technology giants in the benchmark S&P 500 Index is now carrying over into the niche market for dividend futures and options.

item.title: Fed’s Waller Says Stablecoins to Broaden Reach of US Policy
item.description: Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller the spread of stablecoins around the world could amplify the influence of US central-bank policy.

item.title: ECB Should Act Sooner Rather Than Later, Pereira Tells Negócios
item.description: The European Central Bank shouldn’t hesitate to act, given elevated consumer-price pressures, according to Governing Council member Alvaro Santos Pereira.

item.title: AI Bubble Debate Gets Real as Chip Stocks Rally Turns Historic
item.description: Chipmakers are by far the hottest stocks in the market, but their recent surge is lending urgency to the debate over whether investors are buying into an artificial-intelligence bubble that’s due to burst.

item.title: Congo Adds Lithium to Strategic Minerals in Higher Tax Bracket
item.description: Democratic Republic of Congo’s lithium miners could soon be required to triple royalty payments after the government approved the inclusion of several metals to its list of strategic minerals.

item.title: EM’s Record Rally Leads Two Hedge Funds to Turn Away Investors
item.description: There’s so much cash going to emerging markets that a pair of hedge funds that specialize in hard-to-reach debt are starting to turn away investors.

item.title: Indonesia Advances Export Control Plan Despite Uncertainty
item.description: Indonesia is pushing ahead with plans to centralize exports of key commodities starting June 1, casting a cloud of regulatory uncertainty over natural resource producers.

item.title: Singapore’s CapitaLand Sheds 10% of China Staff Amid Downturn
item.description: CapitaLand Investment Ltd. said its workforce in China plunged by about 10%, or 365 people, last year as the asset manager continues to grapple with a major real estate crisis in Asia’s largest economy.

item.title: China Mine Collapse Kills Five, Days After Deadly Shanxi Blast
item.description: Five workers died after a collapse at a mine in southwestern China, underscoring persistent safety issues and increased scrutiny following a gas blast at a coal mine in Shanxi earlier this month that killed at least 82 people.

item.title: African Development Bank Allots $650 Million for Uganda Railroad
item.description: Uganda said the African Development Bank has tentatively allocated about $650 million to partly fund the construction of a railway linking the capital, Kampala, to Malaba at the Kenyan border.

item.title: Saudi Contractor’s Owners Seek Up To $800 Million in Riyadh IPO
item.description: Saudi Arabia’s Mutlaq Al-Ghowairi Contracting Co.’s shareholders are seeking up to 3 billion riyals ($799 million) in a Riyadh initial public offering, in what would be the Gulf’s first major listing this year.

item.title: China Signals Online-Platform Policy Balancing Growth, Oversight
item.description: China signaled it would focus its policy for online platforms on balancing support for growth with enhanced regulatory oversight, according to a commentary in a top-level Communist Party publication.

item.title: Taiwan Vows Not to Be ‘Silenced’ After China Expels NYT Reporter
item.description: Taiwan has condemned the expulsion of a New York Times journalist from China over an interview the US-based newspaper conducted with the self-ruled island’s leader, saying it is common for a president of a democratic country to explain its position.

item.title: China Factory Activity Worsens in Warning Sign for Economy
item.description: China’s factory activity slowed in May as disruptions from a five-day break added to pressures on global demand and input costs from the continuing conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: SpaceX, OpenAI Windfall Fuels Bets on Next-Wave Asian AI Winners
item.description: The hunt is on for companies that could benefit from the tailwinds of an unprecedented wave of stock offerings in the US, and investors are increasingly honing in on the Asian supply chain.

item.title: IMF Chief, Venezuelan Officials Hold Talks on Economic Stability
item.description: International Monetary Fund Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva and Venezuelan economic official Calixto Ortega held talks in Washington, the IMF head’s first in-person meeting with country’s authorities since the fund resumed formal engagement with Venezuela last month.

item.title: US Jobs Report Set to Reveal Solid Growth, Steady Unemployment Rate
item.description: Jobs week is coming up in the US, with a whole slate of indicators on the state of the labor market culminating on Friday with the government’s official report on employment for the month of May.

item.title: Brazil Extends Measures to Limit Fuel Price Hikes by Two Months
item.description: The Brazilian government extended by two months measures to contain the rise in fuel prices triggered by the conflict in Middle East.

item.title: Paramount Is Pulling Every Lever to Sell LBO Debt
item.description: Paramount Skydance Corp. stretched, then stretched, then stretched again in its audacious $110 billion takeover bid for Warner Bros. Discovery Inc.

item.title: SoftBank to Invest Some €75 Billion in AI in France, Reports Say
item.description: SoftBank Group Corp. plans to invest as much as €75 billion ($87 billion) to build artificial intelligence data centers in France, La Tribune and the Financial Times reported.

item.title: Vitol Awarded Namibia Fuel Supply Deal From July to September
item.description: Namibia said it arranged an emergency deal with Vitol Group for three months of fuel supply through September in an effort to mitigate price shocks for oil products caused by the Iran war.

item.title: BP’s High-Stakes Reboot Descended Into an Ugly Boardroom Drama
item.description: When Albert Manifold was hired as chairman of BP Plc, he joked to friends the company needed a “gurrier” — Irish slang for a savvy and scrappy streetfighter — to help turn its fortunes around.

item.title: Fed’s Williams Quips Economist Jobs Are Safe as AI Shift Unfolds
item.description: The implications of artificial intelligence gripped global central bankers in Iceland this week, with one Federal Reserve official joking that it won’t put economists out of business.

item.title: ‘Dinosaur’ Tech Stocks Reborn as AI Fuels $1.7 Trillion Rally
item.description: They were all stars of the dot-com era before fading into the background as the bubble burst and a new generation of tech darlings rose to the forefront. But Dell Technologies Inc., Nokia Oyj and Lenovo Group Ltd. are back with a vengeance thanks to the unrelenting artificial intelligence spending boom.

item.title: Aluminum Under Pressure, While Poland Surges Ahead
item.description: It will take a long time to bring aluminum back.

item.title: AI Dangers Eclipse Nuclear Weapons at Singapore Defense Forum
item.description: The dangers of AI eclipsed those of nuclear weapons at a defense forum discussion on strategic stability in Singapore, as panelists warned it could reduce reaction times to the point where people make rash decisions.

item.title: BOE’s Mann Warns ‘Good Luck’ of Low Inflation Era Has Run Out
item.description: Bank of England rate-setter Catherine Mann warned that the long run of “good luck” central bankers have enjoyed in keeping a lid on inflation has run out as a more shock-prone era sets in.
